coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of green carbon dots as bioimaging agent and drug delivery system for enhanced antioxidant and antibacterial efficacy
coredata.dc:creator: Tejwan, Neeraj
Kundu, Mousumi
Ghosh, Noyel
Chatterjee, Sharmistha
Sharma, Anirudh
Abhishek Singh, Th.
Das, Joydeep
Sil, Parames C.
coredata.dc:description: Now-a-days, synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from renewable sources has attracted considerable attention due to their wide spread availability, lower cost, higher aqueous solubility and more biocompatibility. Here, we have synthesized water soluble CDs with size between 1 and 4 nm from the root extract of red Korean ginseng via simple microwave irradiation. The CDs showed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission and used as a bioimaging agent. The CDs were further loaded with a natural flavonoid, Rutin to check its efficacy as a drug delivery system. The CDs showed a drug loading content of 12.7 % and a sustained drug release behavior at physiological conditions. CDs showed excellent free radical scavenging activity both in cell free systems and intra-cellular conditions. Besides, loading with the drug further improved the intra-cellular radical scavenging activity. The CDs and the nanohybrids (Rutin-loaded CDs) displayed very little toxicity upto the concentration of 100 μg/mL in normal kidney epithelial cells. The CDs and the nanohybrids also showed a dose-dependent antibacterial effect against both gram negative as well as gram positive bacteria under light exposure as well as dark conditions due to membrane damage. However; under light exposure, the antibacterial activity of CDs and the nanohybrids was further increased due to the production of excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) and subsequent DNA damage. Overall, our synthesized green CDs can be considered as a promising candidate for the biomedical applications due to its facile synthesis, low cytotoxicity, excellent fluorescent properties and potential as drug delivery systems.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of green carbon dots as bioimaging agent and drug delivery system for enhanced antioxidant and antibacterial efficacy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Now-a-days, synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from renewable sources has attracted considerable attention due to their wide spread availability, lower cost, higher aqueous solubility and more biocompatibility. Here, we have synthesized water soluble CDs with size between 1 and 4 nm from the root extract of red Korean ginseng via simple microwave irradiation. The CDs showed excitation-dependent fluorescence emission and used as a bioimaging agent. The CDs were further loaded with a natural flavonoid, Rutin to check its efficacy as a drug delivery system. The CDs showed a drug loading content of 12.7 % and a sustained drug release behavior at physiological conditions. CDs showed excellent free radical scavenging activity both in cell free systems and intra-cellular conditions. Besides, loading with the drug further improved the intra-cellular radical scavenging activity. The CDs and the nanohybrids (Rutin-loaded CDs) displayed very little toxicity upto the concentration of 100 μg/mL in normal kidney epithelial cells. The CDs and the nanohybrids also showed a dose-dependent antibacterial effect against both gram negative as well as gram positive bacteria under light exposure as well as dark conditions due to membrane damage. However; under light exposure, the antibacterial activity of CDs and the nanohybrids was further increased due to the production of excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) and subsequent DNA damage. Overall, our synthesized green CDs can be considered as a promising candidate for the biomedical applications due to its facile synthesis, low cytotoxicity, excellent fluorescent properties and potential as drug delivery systems.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoparticle technology or nanotechnology is the study of nanomaterials in the field of electronics, medicine and agriculture. Carbon is the most abundant material on the earth having various allotropes like diamond, fullerene and graphite. Nanodiamonds, fullerenes, carbon dots (CDs), graphenes and carbon nanofibers are the new class of carbon nanomaterials, and have attracted much attention in the field of nanotechnology. CDs are the newly discovered member among the family with size less than 10 nm [1]. These nanomaterials are enriched with superior properties like excellent quantum yield, superior biocompatibility, good water solubility, higher degree of cell permeability and low cytotoxicity [2] which makes them useful to be applied in the fields of biology and medicine [3]. CDs can be synthesized using various chemical and natural precursors via both top down as well as bottom up approaches. However, most of the researchers are widely using chemical precursors to synthesize CDs [4,5]; therefore, there is much need of using eco-friendly techniques and sources to synthesize CDs in order to avoid their adverse effects. Some of the researchers have started using natural precursors for the synthesis of CDs due to their high renewability, low cost, and better biocompatibility [6–9]. Various sources like plant materials, beverages, fruits and vegetables have been used to synthesize carbon dots [10–12]. Among those precursors, researchers are focusing more on the plant derived CDs.

Since ancient times, plants have not only been used as a food source but also been used to treat various diseases; therefore, several herbal medicines have been considered as the traditional methods of remedy [13,14]. Sometimes, the plant extract shows more efficacy and low toxicity in comparison to the commercial drugs [15]. Plants are enriched with different secondary metabolites such as flavonoids, glycosides, tannins, alkaloids, terpenoids etc. which are responsible for showing their medicinal properties [16]. Extraction of the biologically active compounds from plants depends on the solvent used in the extraction procedure [17]. Different solvents such as alcohols, ether, ethyl acetate, chloroform and water are commonly used for the extraction purpose, and obviously water is the safest one among them [18]. Therefore, researchers are now showing their efforts for synthesizing CDs from the aqueous extract of several medicinal herbs [19]. Ginseng is one of the among medicinal herbs [20,21]; therefore, we used the aqueous root extract of red Korean ginseng for the synthesis of CDs.

Panax ginseng Meyer, also called Korean ginseng is a plant which has been used as the traditional medicinal herb in the eastern Asian countries such as Korea, Japan and China [22,23]. When the fresh Korean ginseng becomes red in color upon heating, it is called red Korean ginseng (RKG) [24]. The main constituents of RKG are ginsenosides, flavones, amino acids, acetylenic compounds, pyran derivatives and peptide glycans [25]. Ginsenosides have various medicinal properties like anticancer, antioxidant, antibacterial, anti-inflammatory, anti-diabetic, anti-stress, and anti-apoptotic [26]. Besides, RKG has also been widely studied and used for the treatment or improving the conditions of the various human disease [27,28]. However, most of the researchers have used RKG extract for the synthesis of some inorganic nanoparticles [29,30] and only one report showed the use of ginsenosides for the preparation of CDs with anticancer potential [31].

Recently CDs have attracted the attention of researchers as a drug delivery system [32,33] to increase the bioavailability of the drugs into the target organs while reducing its adverse effects to normal tissue. CDs have high surface area with sp2 core within it, and can conjugate various hydrophobic biomolecules via π- π* stacking or electrostatic interactions, thereby increasing their solubility in the aqueous media [2,34]. Besides, drugs can also be conjugated via covalent bonding with the functional groups present on the surface of CDs. Furthermore, the drug delivery pathway can also be tracked due to the fluorescent nature of the CDs [35]. However, there is only few studies showing the use of CDs as a drug delivery system as most of the studies have focused on its optical properties [2,36,37].

In the present study, we have synthesized a green CDs from aqueous root extract of RKG using microwave assisted heating and checked their potentials to be used as an antioxidant, antibacterial and bioimaging agents. Besides, we have also tested whether those CDs could be used as a drug delivery system for a hydrophobic natural flavonoid, Rutin. Rutin is an important part of our regular diet and has profound nutraceutical benefits. It is abundantly found in the several vegetables and fruits such as, asparagus, buckwheat, figs, apple, grapes, plum. orange, and also found in some beverages such as green tea, black tea and elderflower tea [38]. Rutin exhibits multiple pharmacological actions and hence can be used for the treatment of several diseases like hypertension, cancer, hypercholesterolemia, diabetes, etc. Besides, it is also well-known for its cytoprotective, antioxidant, antibacterial, anticarcinogenic, neuroprotective, vasoprotective and cardioprotective activities [39]. However, the pharmacological actions of rutin are greatly limited by their low bioavailability due to poor aqueous solubility and low cell permeability [40]. In order to overcome those limitations, CDs were used as drug delivery vehicle to improve its pharmacological actions. Since both ginseng and rutin possess anti-oxidant & anti-bacterial activities; therefore, we hypothesized that the nanohybrids (CDs-RUT) would exhibit higher antioxidant and antibacterial activities compared with free rutin by increasing the hydrophilicity and pharmacological actions of the hydrophobic drug.

Potassium ferricyanide, trichloroacteic acid, ferric chloride, acetate buffer, hydrogen peroxide, hydrochloric acid, rutin, ascorbic acid, sodium hydroxide and dimethyl sulphoxide were purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd. (Maharashtra, India), while other reagents, such as ammonium molybdate, ferrous sulphate (96%), 1,1-diphenyl-2-picrylhydrazyl, monobasic and dibasic potassium phosphates were obtained from Central Drug House Pvt. Ltd. (New Delhi, India). Propidium iodide (PI) and 3,3-dihexyloxacarbocyanine iodide (DiOC6) dye were bought from Merck India Company (Maharashtra, India). Red Korean Ginseng (RKG) was purchased online from amazon.in. All the chemicals were used without further purification. Millipore system purified deionized water of conductivity 18 Ω cm−1 has been used in this experiment.

CDs were synthesized from the aqueous root extract of red Korean Ginseng (RKG) via microwave assisted heating method [41]. The extract was heated in a domestic microoven (IFB, Bangalore, India) with a tunable power of 700 W having frequency of 2450 MHz. We have heated the extract for 2 mins in every cycles for a total 7 cycles with cooling intervals of 2 min after each cycle. Then, the formed CDs were suspended in water, dialysed with a 3500 Da MWCO membrane and lyophilized by employing Allied Frost lyophilizer (New Delhi, India) to get pure CDs in powder form.

The aqueous solution of CDs was mixed with RUT dissolved in DMSO in a 1:1 mass ratio. The loading of RUT was done overnight with stirring at room temperature. After that the solution was dialyzed for 24 hrs to remove the unbound drugs. The purified drug-loaded CDs or the nanohybrids (CDs-RUT) were lyophilized to get CDs-RUT in powder form. We have checked the drug loading content by thermo gravimetric analysis by a thermogravimetric analyser from Perkin Elmer company (Pyris 1; MA, USA). The drug loading content (DLC) was also determined by the UV–Vis spectroscopy. Absorbance of RUT at 340 nm was checked before and after the drug loading, and the DLC was calculated with the help of calibration curve obtained by a series of RUT solutions with known concentrations under the same conditions. The drug loading content was calculated by using the formula as shown below:

Transmission electron microscope (FEI; FP-5022 Tecnai G2 20 S-TWIN, USA) was used to know the size and morphology of the prepared particles. X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern was analysed by Rigaku corporation (Smartlab 9 kW, Tokyo, Japan) X-ray diffractometer. Raman spectra were obtained using Invia Raman manufactured by Renishaw company (UK). The fluorescence and uv–vis spectra of the prepared particles were determined by using Thermo Fischer Scientific (Varioskan LUX 3020–561; Waltham, USA) spectrofluorimeter and Wensar (LMSP-UV1900; Chennai, India) spectrophotometer respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) instrument of Agilent technologies (L1600312; CA, USA) had been used to record the FTIR spectra in the range from 500 to 4000 cm−1. The hydrodynamic size of the synthesized nanoparticles was investigated by Dynamic Light Scattering instrument (Delsa, Nano C particle analyser; CA, USA).
Photostability of CDs was checked by irradiating aqueous solution of CDs (1.5 mg/mL) with 100 W LED bulb. The fluorescence intensity was checked thereafter at an interval of 1 hr.

We have investigated the time dependent release of RUT from CDs-RUT nanohybrids in phosphate buffer saline (PBS) of pH = 7.4. We first dispersed the nanohybrids in the buffer with a concentration of 2 mg/mL by sonication. These solutions were then put into a dialysis membrane of 3500 Da and the drug release was monitored by taking out the aqueous solution into which the dialysis membrane was floated under stirring condition at 37 °C. The drug release was checked after 6, 12, 24, and 48 hrs respectively by measuring the absorbance at 340 nm using an UV–Vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent images of the synthesized nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescent images of the synthesized CDs have been taken by using Nikon fluorescence microscope. We first dispersed the CDs in PBS with a concentration of 1 mg/mL by sonication. After that, a drop of the above solution was placed on a clean glass slide. Bright field, FITC, DAPI and RITC filters were used to capture the image by using 20X objective lens of fluorescent microscope.

CDs uptake was checked in the human breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7) which were plated in a 35 mm cell culture dish with a sterile glass cover slip. On the next day, the cells were treated with 50 μg/mL of CDs for 6 hrs. After that the cells were washed with 1X PBS, the coverslips were mounted on a glass slide using a mounting media with DAPI, and the images were captured using the DAPI and FITC filters by the fluorescent microscope.

The ability of the ginseng derived CDs to neutralize the DPPH radicals has been examined by the method as published by Shimada et al. with some modifications [42]. The stock solution of the DPPH was prepared by dissolving 2 mg DPPH in 100 mL ethanol solution and was kept in the refrigerator until its use. Aqueous solutions of CDs with various concentrations ranging from 50 μg/mL to 600 μg/mL were mixed with a 2 mL DPPH solution and incubated for 30 min at 37 °C in dark. After that the absorbance were measured at 517 nm and the results were presented as % inhibition by using the formula;
%

The ability of CDs to scavenge H2O2 free radicals has been investigated by using the method as reported by Ruch et al. with minor modifications [43]. Various concentrations of ginseng derived CDs (50 μg/mL − 700 μg/mL) were mixed with 43 mM H2O2 in 1 M PBS and incubated for 10 min at 37 °C in dark·H2O2 scavenging activity of CDs was evaluated by taking absorbance at 230 nm against the blank solution. % scavenging activity was calculated by using the formula:
%

A control

%

Reducing power of the CDs was checked against ferric ions by the procedure reported by Vijayalakshmi and Rukmani [44]. 150 μL CDs with different concentrations were mixed with 1 mL buffer (pH = 6.6), 1 mL of (1%) potassium ferricyanide and 1 mL 10% TCA, and then kept in an incubator for 20 min at 50 °C. After that the solution was centrifuged at 3000 rpm for 10 min and to the supernatant, 1 mL distilled water and 250 μL 1% FeCl3 were added. The mixture was again kept for another 10 min and the absorbance of the samples were measured at 700 nm. In this assay, the colour of the samples became dark upon increasing the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+.

Ferric reducing anti-oxidant potential of the CDs were investigated by the method reported by Ahmed at al. with slight changes [45]. At first the FRAP reagents were prepared by mixing 40 mm TPTZ, acetate buffer and 0.1% FeCl3 in the ratio 1:10:1 and then added to the CDs with different concentrations. After that the resultant mixture were kept in the incubator for 15 min at 50 °C in dark and measured the absorbance at 593 nm. In this assay, Fe3+ transformed to Fe2+ which was confirmed by the blue-coloured product (FeII-TPTZ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of intracellular reactive oxygenated species (ROS) in normal cells
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the intracellular ROS level, the human normal kidney epethelial (NKE) cells were first exposed to 100 μg/mL of CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT nanohybrid for 12 hrs followed by addition of tertiarybutyl hydroperoxide (TBHP) for 1 hrs. After that, cells were scraped, centrifuged for 5 min at the speed of 300X g at room temperature, resuspended in PBS, and then incubated with the probe (2, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate) H2DCFDA with a concentration of 2 μM for 25 min at 37 °C. Finally, the green fluorescence intensity of the cells was checked by using a BD-FACS Calibur Flow Cytometry System using a bandpass filter of 525 nm [46].

The cytotoxicity of the CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT were measured against the human normal kidney epethelial (NKE) cells by standard MTT assay [47,48]. Firstly, the cells were plated in a 96 well plate having density of 1. 25 × 105 cells/well inside the incubator. After 24 hrs of incubation under 5% CO2 at 37 °C, cells were treated with different concentrations (10–100 μg/mL) of the CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT for 24 hrs. 100 μL/well of MTT with concentration of 0.5 mg/mL was added to the well after removing the media. At the end, 100 μL of DMSO solution per well was added and incubated for another 4 h. The absorbance was taken at 570 nm by using microtiter plate reader and the cell viability (%) was calculated by using the formula:
Cell
v i a b i l i t y
%
=
A
sample
/
A
control
× 100
%
where, Asample = Absorbance of the sample
Acontrol = Absorbance of the control

Gram negative, Escherichia coli (E. coli) and gram positive, Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) bacteria were used to check the dose dependent antibacterial activity of the CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT. This assay was performed according to the protocol mentioned in the report with slight modifications [47]. At first, 10 μL of inoculums were added into a fresh culture medium. Then different concentrations (0–100 μg/mL) of CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT were added to the medium and incubated for 18 hrs in a shaker at 37 °C either under dark or light exposure conditions. In the light exposure experiments, 36 W LED light source was used and placed at 15 cm from the bacterial solution. Luria–Bertani (LB) media consist of only particles are considered as blank whereas, LB with only inoculums is treated as controls. Finally, the growth of the bacteria was calculated by taking the absorbance at 600 nm by using a spectrophotometer.

The bacterial cells were first treated with 100 μg/mL of CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT nanohybrid for 18 h. Bacterial DNAs were extracted thereafter from the cell pellet using Bacterial DNA extraction Kit (Qiagen, Genmany). 100 mg DNA were run on 1% agarose gel and electrophoresis was carried out at 110 V for 30 min [49]. Bands were detected by staining with ethidium bromide.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of intracellular ROS in bacteria
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the intracellular ROS level, the bacterial cells were first exposed to CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT nanohybrid for 18 hrs with the concentrations of 100 μg/mL either under dark or light exposure conditions. After that, cell suspension was centrifuged for 5 min at the speed of 300X g at room temperature, resuspended in PBS, and then incubated with the probe (2, 7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate) H2DCFDA with a concentration of 2 μM for 25 min at 37 °C. Finally, the green fluorescence intensity of the cells was checked by using a spectrofluorimeter at an excitation wavelength of 495 nm and emission wavelength of 525 nm [46,50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of cell membrane potential and permeability
ce:section.ce:para: Membrane potential was checked by using (3, 3′-dihexyloxacarbocyanine) DiOC6 [43]. The bacterial strains were exposed to 100 μg mL−1 of CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT for 18 hrs followed by washing with PBS and incubation with 20 μM DiOC6 [51]. Membrane permeability was evaluated by using propidium iodide (PI), at a concentration of 9 μM [52]. The green and red fluorescence of the bacterial cells were measured by using a spectrofluorimeter at Ex/ Em = 535/617 nm and Ex/ Em = 495/525 nm, respectively [53].
All the results have shown as mean ± SD after performing each experiment at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and time dependent drug release
ce:section.ce:para: Drug loading content was found to be approximately 14 % via TGA (Fig. 2 d). We have also utilized UV–Vis spectroscopy to check the drug loading content. Approximately 12.7 % drug loading content was observed. We have also checked the time dependent RUT release from the nanohybrids under physiological condition and at different intervals of time (Fig. 2 e). We have found that the free drug concentration was increased in the suspension (pH = 7.4) with time (Fig. S 3a & b), and the maximum drug concentration achieved was around 1.5 μg/mL after 48 hrs. After 48 hrs, there was no significant change in the release.

Fig. 3
a displayed the UV–Vis spectra of CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT. CDs showed the absorption peaks at 285 nm, 340 nm and 370 nm. Drug showed the characteristic peaks at 255 nm and 350 nm. However, in the CDs-RUT, the parent peaks were shifted from their original positions due to the interactions between CDs and RUT.

The fluorescence spectra of the CDs were shown in the Fig. 3 b. CDs showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission within the excitation range of 320 to 500 nm. Maximum emission was observed when they were excited at 380 nm. We have also checked the photostability of the CDs and no significant change in the fluorescence intensity was observed upto 4 hrs under the continuous irradiation of 100 W LED light, thereby indicating high photostability of the CDs ( Fig. S4
). Fluorescence microscopic analysis has shown the intense blue, green and red colors coming from the CDs when excited at their respective excitation wavelength (Fig. 3 c). Besides, we have also checked the CDs uptake in MCF-7 human breast cancer cell line via fluorescence microscopic analysis and we observed intense green fluorescence coming from the cells upon CDs treatment (Fig. 3 d). These results are indicating the probable use of the CDs as bio-imaging agents.

DPPH produces a dark purple colour in solution due to the extensive delocalization of the free radical, and the colour gets faded in presence of antioxidants which neutralise the free radicals. When the DPPH solution was incubated with increasing concentrations of CDs, the colour intensity was gradually decreased, thereby revealing their antioxidant nature. We found that CDs showed 85.4 % scavenging activity at the concentration of 600 μg/mL as shown in Fig. 4
a, and no significant changes were observed thereafter.

H2O2 is highly important radical that can be produced inside the cells. Intact H2O2 shows the absorbance peak at 230 nm, the absorbance value gets decreased upon its conversion into O2 and H2O in presence of antioxidants. Our synthesized CDs have shown 77–87 % H2O2 scavenging activity within the concentration range of 50–700 μg/mL as depicted in Fig. 4 b.

The reducing power of any antioxidant can be determined by its electron donating ability to Fe3+ ions. When the test solution containing potassium ferricyanide was treated with CDs, it was reduced to potassium ferrocyanide which produced a dark green color in presence of Fe3+ ions. When the absorbance of the Prussian blue complex was measured, we found that CDs showed a concentration–dependent rise in color intensity upto a concentration of 1000 μg/mL (Fig. 4 c).

This is another method to check the electron donating ability of any antioxidant molecules via the reduction of Fe3+ to Fe2+. When CDs were added to (FeIII-TPTZ), it was reduced to a blue-coloured product (FeII-TPTZ). We have also measured the absorbance of the complex formed, and observed that the colour intensity was increased with increasing concentration of the added CDs upto a concentration of 700 μg/mL (Fig. 4 d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intracellular reactive oxidative species (ROS) scavenging activity
ce:section.ce:para: Intracellular ROS levels were measured by using FACS analysis in the NKE cells. Cells were pre-treated with CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT for 18 hrs with the concentration of 40 μg/mL followed by treatment with TBHP for 1 hr. We observed that TBHP exposure increased the intracellular ROS level as indicated by the increased fluorescence as compared to the control cells ( Fig. 4 e). However, pre-treatment with CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT reduced the ROS levels, thereby indicating their antioxidant nature ( Fig. 4 e). CDs-RUT showed the maximum level of ROS neutralization effect among the tree groups.

Cytotoxicity of the as prepared nanoformulations, viz CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT were checked in the NKE cells. The cells were treated with the nanoformulations within the concentration range from 10 to 100 μg/mL for 24 hrs, and the cell viability was measured by using MTT assay. We have observed that CDs were imparting very less toxicity and only around 15% cells underwent death at the maximum dose used (Fig. 4 f). Similarly, RUT and CDs-RUT also did not cause any severe toxicity and more than 80% cells were alive at the maximum dose of treatment (Fig. 4 f).

We have checked the antibacterial effects of the CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT nanohybrids in both gram positive (S. aureus) and gram negative (E. coli), bacteria under light irradiation as well as dark. We have observed that although CDs and RUT alone could not significantly inhibit the bacterial growth under dark but the CDs-RUT nanohybrid showed a dose-dependent synergistic effect in inhibiting the bacterial growth in both the strains (Fig. 5
a & b). However, the antibacterial activity of CDs, and CDs-RUT nanohybrids were further increased under visible light irradiation.

We have also compared the MIC values among the groups under dark as well as light illuminating conditions. Under dark, the values are 0.54, 0.49 and 0.17 mg/mL for the gram positive bacteria and 0.55, 0.55 and 0.18 mg/mL for the gram negative bacteria for CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT treated groups respectively. On the other hand; CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT showed MIC values of 0.26, 0.47 and 0.10 mg/mL for gram positive strain, and 0.27, 0.52 and 0.15 mg/mL for gram negative strain respectively under light illuminating condition.

We have further checked the DNA integrity in both gram-positive (S. aureus) and gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria under light illuminating condition (Fig. 5 c & d) since both CDs and CDs-RUT nanohybrids exhibited higher antibacterial activities under visible light irradiation. Treatment with the nanoformulations significantly reduced the band intensity of intact genomic DNA, and CDs-RUT nanohybrids displayed highest extend of DNA degradation among the groups. Besides, higher extend of DNA degradation was observed against gram-positive bacteria.

We have also checked whether the nanoformulations could induce ROS formation within the bacteria under dark as well as light illuminating conditions (Fig. 6
a & b). We observed that CDs and CDs-RUT nanohybrids caused 3.17 and 4.95 folds increase in ROS levels in the gram positive bacteria, whereas 2.47 and 4.73 folds increase in ROS levels in gram negative bacteria respectively under light illuminating condition with respect to their control groups. The ROS production ability of RUT did not change significantly under dark and light illuminating conditions. However, CDs caused enhanced ROS production under visible light irradiation as compared to dark.

Finally, the antibacterial activity of CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT has been checked by evaluating the bacterial membrane dysfunction in both gram positive and gram negative bacteria. Cell membrane permeability and membrane potential were evaluated by fluorescent dye uptake, e.g. PI and DiOC6. Higher PI uptake (increased red fluorescence) by the cells indicates membrane disruption, i.e. increased membrane permeability and decreased membrane integrity following incubation with CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT (Fig. 6 c & d). Besides, CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT also caused decreased green fluorescence of DiOC6, thereby indicating bacterial membrane depolarization (Fig. 6 e & f). CDs-RUT exhibited maximum membrane dysfunction, and the damaging effect was even higher under light illuminating condition. The extend of membrane dysfunction was not significantly changed for RUT under dark and light illuminating conditions, whereas CDs caused enhanced membrane dysfunction under visible light irradiation as compared to dark.

In the present study, we have prepared a biocompatible green CDs from the aqueous root extract of red Korean Ginseng and investigated its applications as a bioimaging agent as well as a drug carrier for the delivery of a hydrophobic bioactive flavonoid, rutin (RUT) for its increased antioxidant and antibacterial efficacy.

The as synthesized CDs were characterized by using various physical techniques such as TEM, DLS, EDX, XRD, and FTIR spectroscopy analyses. TEM images showed nearly spherical shape of the synthesized CDs with diameter within the range of 1–4 nm. The observed lattice spacing at 0.337 nm corresponded to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon [55]. However, the DLS data showed a slight increase in the size of CDs. TEM analysis is normally performed in dry and static form, but in DLS measurements, the hydrodynamic size of nanoparticles is investigated in hydrated form. Hence, the hydrodynamic diameter of those nanoparticles is higher than TEM size due to the extensive hydration/salvation [56]. The drug loading over CD surface was supported by the increased hydrodynamic size of the CDs-RUT nanohybrids in comparison to the CDs. The EDX spectrum of CDs revealed the presence of carbon and oxygen; while the XRD pattern showed a broad peak at 2θ = 24.1 which corresponded to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon, thereby indicating their amorphous nature [57–59]. Raman spectra of CDs exhibited two different bands at 1384 cm−1 and 1589 cm−1, which are attributed to sp2 carbon (G-band) and sp3 defects (D-band) in the synthesized CDs [60]. Zeta potential values are the indicator of surface charge of nanomaterials and their colloidal stability [61]. The CDs showed the zeta potential value of −1.67 mV which is attributed to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on their surface. The zeta potential values of RUT and CDs-RUT were −0.543 mV and −10.34 mV respectively. The higher negative zeta potential value of CDs-RUT is reflecting the presence of both negatively charged CDs and RUT. The increased negative charge of CDs-RUT is advantageous in avoiding nonspecific interaction with serum/protein components, thereby rendering increased colloidal stability in biological media.

FTIR spectrum of the as prepared CDs-RUT nanohybrids showed several characteristic peaks of pure RUT in conjunction with CDs, thereby further confirming the drug attachment onto the CD surface. CDs showed the major transmittance peak/bands at 3323 cm−1, 2925 cm−1, 1596–1700 cm−1 and 1017 cm−1 which corresponds to the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl or amine groups, C-H groups, C = O and aromatic C = C bonds, and C-O bonds respectively [54]. Pure RUT showed peaks at 1652 cm−1 , 1504 cm−1 and 1358 cm−1 which could be the characteristic peaks of mixed C = O and C = C bonds, symmetric stretching vibrations of aromatic ring and C-O stretching respectively. Some transmittance peaks at 3621 cm−1, 3419 cm−1 could be assigned to O-H stretching while peaks at 2937 cm−1, 2919 cm−1 might be due to the C-H stretching [62].

Further the TGA was performed to check the amount of drug loaded onto the CD surface and it was found to be around 14% by weight. Later we have also checked whether the CDs-RUT nanohybrid could release the drug under physiological condition as the free form of any drug is required to show its pharmacological action. We have observed that the nanohybrid was broken down under physiological condition and leading to the release of RUT after 6 hrs. The concentration of the drug in the release media was around 1.3 μg/mL after 6 hrs which is very close to the drug concentration (1.5 μg/mL) achieved after 48 hrs, thereby indicating a very fast release of the drug to show its quick pharmacological effects.

The optical properties of the CDs were evaluated via using UV–VIS and fluorescence spectroscopic as well as fluorescence microscopic analyses. CDs showed the characteristic absorption peaks at 285 nm due to the π-π* transitions of aromatic sp2 groups whereas peaks observed at 340 nm and 370 nm were attributed to the π-π* and n-π* transitions of carbonyl groups respectively [41,63]. The CDs-RUT nanohybrid showed all the characteristic peaks of CDs and RUT with slight shifting due to overlap or interaction between the functional groups of CDs and RUT [64]. The fluorescence nature of the CDs was evaluated by analyzing its fluorescence spectra and fluorescence microscopic analyses. CDs showed an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission with a red shift when the excitation wavelength changes from 320 to 500 nm, and the maximum fluorescence intensity was observed when they were excited from 380 to 400 nm. The CDs showed quantum yield value of 8 % with no significant change in fluorescence intensity with high salt concentrations upto 1 M [41] as well as under the continuous irradiation of 100 W LED light for 4 hrs. Besides, the CDs also displayed a steady decrease in fluorescence intensity from pH 3 to 12 because of the different deprotonation degrees of the hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups present on CDs surface at different pH [41]. Here, we have also correlated the optical properties of the CDs with previously reported CDs synthesized from natural sources in Table 1
[65–72]. The CDs also showed blue, green and red fluorescence under ultra-violet, blue and green light excitation respectively, thereby indicating the probable use of the CDs as bioimaging agents [73,74]. We have further proved the possibility of using CDs for bio-imaging using mammalian cells which showed intense green fluorescence when incubated with CDs.

Next, we have checked the free radical scavenging activities of the CDs in cell free system via DPPH radical and H2O2 scavenging activity as well as ferric reducing power and FRAP assay. CDs showed a dose-dependent DPPH radical and H2O2 scavenging activity. Besides, CDs exhibited a strong electron donating ability as indicated by the dose-dependent reduction of ferric to ferrous ions in presence of CDs. All these above results indicated that our synthesized CDs have profound antioxidant potentials due to its highly efficient electron donating or reducing ability [75]. The antioxidant nature or free radical scavenging activity of CDs is attributed to radical adduct formation at sp2 sites [76]. Higher the number of sp2 sites, higher will be the scavenging activity [77]. Carbon nanomaterials with lower number of sp2 sites show their scavenging activity through electron transfer if the graphene core is doped with electron rich elements as well as by hydrogen transfer from the functional groups having hydrogen-donating ability [76,77].

Inspired by its efficient antioxidant properties in cell free system, we have also checked whether it could show the equally efficient antioxidant properties within cells. For that, we have first pre-treated the human normal kidney epithelial cells with CDs followed by treatment with a known ROS inducer, TBHP. We have observed that CDs could inhibit the ROS formation to a larger extent induced by TBHP within the cells, thereby indicating its cellular antioxidant nature. Moreover, treatment with the CDs-RUT nanohybrids could almost neutralize the ROS and this effect is even higher than the free drug, RUT due to the combined effects of the CDs and drugs. These results indicated that the pharmacological actions of the drugs were retained even after release from the nanohybrids. Therefore, our synthesized CDs can not only strengthen the antioxidant defence mechanism of cells, but also increase the pharmacological actions of natural bioactive hydrophobic antioxidant molecules by increasing its hydrophilicity. Further, we have checked the maximum tolerated dose of the CDs and CDs-RUT nanohybrids in normal cells and we observed that the nanoformulations did not cause any severe toxicity and more than 80% cells were alive within the concentration range from 10 to 100 μg/mL when treated for 24 hrs.

Finally, the antibacterial activities of the CDs, RUT and CDs-RUT nanohybrids were checked against both gram negative (E. coli) and gram positive (S. aureus) bacteria. Antibacterial activity of the ginseng extracts has previously been reported [29,30,78]. Growth kinetic studies showed that CDs-RUT exhibited a synergistic growth inhibition against both the stains, and the growth inhibition was even higher under light illuminating condition as compared to dark. CDs displayed a higher antibacterial effect under light exposure, whereas RUT did not show much difference under both the conditions. The insignificant antibacterial activity of RUT has also been reported by several other researchers [79,80]. Besides, CDs and CDs-RUT nanohybrids caused extensive DNA degradation in both types of bacteria under light illuminating condition as compared with RUT. However, we did not observe any DNA laddering for the CDs and CDs-RUT treated samples, indicating the removal of small DNA fragments from the agarose gel.

Further, the intracellular ROS formation and bacterial membrane dysfunctions were checked to investigate the mechanism behind the antibacterial effects of the nanoformulations [76,81–83], since flavonoids are known to cause oxidative stress as well as membrane damage in bacteria [51]. Besides, the anti- and pro-oxidant behaviour of CDs in the absence or presence of light has also been reported earlier [84,85]. Being a semiconductor nanomaterial, CDs generate photo-excited electron-hole pairs upon light irradiation and further produce ROS such as superoxide radicals, singlet oxygens and hydroxyl radicals. The photoexcited electron in the conduction band combines with molecular oxygen to produce superoxide radicals (.O2 –), whereas the hole in the valence band reacts with water molecules to produce hydroxyl radicals (.OH). Besides, singlet oxygens (1O2) are also generated by the photoexcited CDs via both electron transfer and energy transfer pathways [84,85]. Flavonoids are behaving as potent antioxidant in normal mammalian cells, but known to cause oxidative stress inside bacterial cells and show antibacterial activity [51,86]. Therefore, the flavonoids can show either antioxidant or prooxidant nature depending on the cell types. In our present study we have observed that CDs could significantly enhance the bacterial ROS levels under light illuminating condition, whereas RUT alone was able to enhance the ROS level under dark. Besides, CDs and RUT also triggered membrane depolarization and augmented membrane permeability, thereby indicating bacterial membrane dysfunction under both dark and light irradiations. However, the CDs-RUT nanohybrids showed the maximum effect in producing ROS and damaging bacterial membrane via the synergistic effect of both CDs and RUT. Therefore, we propose that the insignificant antibacterial effect of CDs in dark is only due to the physical damage to cell membrane; whereas under light illuminating condition, the enhanced antibacterial effect of CDs is due to the increased ROS generation which subsequently enhance membrane damage [76,87].

In conclusion, we have used a green and renewable source, viz aqueous root extract of red Korean ginseng to synthesize biocompatible CDs by a facile, low cost microwave irradiation method without using any passivating agent. Due to the presence of O-containing multifunctional groups, CDs were hydrophilic in nature. They showed multicolour emission (blue, green and red) when excited at ultraviolet, blue and green excitation light respectively and used as bio-imaging agent. Our results also revealed that CDs have excellent antioxidant property due to its efficient electron donating ability, and scavenge ROS both in extracellular and intracellular conditions. Besides, CDs could also be used as a carrier for the delivery of hydrophobic bioactive molecules and increase their pharmacological actions (antioxidant, antibacterial) by increasing their hydrophilicity. We also observed that our synthesized CDs could be used as anti-oxidant or pro-oxidant agents depending on the absence or presence of visible light source. Taken together, our synthesized green CDs can be considered as a promising candidate for the biomedical applications due to its facile synthesis, low cytotoxicity, excellent fluorescent properties and potential as drug delivery systems.
